Q1: I tried to keep a consistent routine, usually studying in the evenings after my part-time work and on weekends. With limited resources at home, I had to be strategic about when I could access the internet and materials. I would block out about three hours most weekday evenings for reading and writing notes, though honestly my motivation wasn't always there. I relied heavily on writing things down repeatedly to help concepts stick, which takes time but works for me.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied depending on what was due. I tried to maintain some regularity, but when deadlines approached, everything else fell apart and I'd study much more intensely. Some weeks I barely touched the course materials because work demanded more hours or I was just feeling too overwhelmed. The anxiety of falling behind would eventually push me to catch up, but it wasn't a healthy cycle.
Q3: Usually it was checking if there were new announcements or upcoming deadlines that prompted me to log in. Sometimes I'd get a notification about a discussion post from a peer and that would remind me to engage. Honestly, fear of missing something important was a big motivator. When I was feeling particularly low, even logging in felt like a huge effort, but I'd force myself because I knew I couldn't afford to fall too far behind.
Q4: For a while in the middle of the semester, I got into a rhythm of logging in every morning before my day started, just to check what needed attention. The peer support group I was part of helped establish this because we'd remind each other about things. But when my stress peaked, that routine broke down completely. It never felt completely natural, more like something I had to consciously maintain.
Q5: The written materials and lecture transcripts were most useful to me because I learn best through reading and writing. I could download them and annotate them extensively, which helped information stick. The discussion forums were also valuable when peers explained concepts in different ways than the instructor. Video content was less helpful because I couldn't easily take notes from it and my internet wasn't always reliable enough for streaming.
Q6: I often skipped the video lectures or only watched portions of them because they took too long and I couldn't always buffer them properly with my connection. Some of the optional readings felt overwhelming when I was already struggling to complete required work. I also delayed interactive quizzes because they made my anxiety spike, even though I knew they'd help me prepare for exams.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see a direct connection to the exam or assignments. If I could read through it, take notes, and feel like I understood something better afterward, that was worthwhile. Activities that required a lot of time investment with unclear benefits felt like luxuries I couldn't afford. When peers recommended something as helpful, that also made me more likely to engage with it.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I rarely had time or energy for. Required activities carried this weight of anxiety because I knew they affected my grade, which made them stressful but also prioritized. Optional things often got pushed aside when I was overwhelmed, even if part of me knew they might help. There was guilt associated with skipping optional materials, but survival mode meant focusing on what was mandatory.
Q9: Absolutely, most of my actual studying happened offline. I would download PDFs and readings, print what I could afford to at the library, and then study from physical copies at home. I filled notebooks with handwritten summaries and practice answers. My best studying happened with just my notes, a pen, and quiet time away from screens. The LMS was more like a portal to get materials than where learning actually happened.
Q10: There were many nights where I'd spend hours reviewing my handwritten notes and rewriting key concepts without ever touching the LMS. When preparing for the exam, I created elaborate study guides from downloaded materials that I reviewed repeatedly offline. Sometimes I'd discuss course content with my peer support group through messaging apps rather than the LMS forums. My real engagement often happened in these invisible spaces.
Q11: Yes, this was my standard approach. I'd download a reading or lecture transcript once, then work with it offline for days or even weeks. My internet access isn't always reliable, so downloading everything I could during one session and then studying offline was essential. I'd only return to the LMS when I needed new materials or had to submit something.
Q12: There were several weeks when my anxiety became almost paralyzing and engaging with anything academic felt impossible. When work increased my hours unexpectedly, I had to choose between earning money and studying. Feeling sad and unmotivated made even simple tasks feel monumental. During those times, I'd go days without logging in even though assignments were piling up, which only made the anxiety worse.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted from work, I'd log into the LMS and just stare at the screen without really absorbing anything. Confusion about certain topics made me avoid those modules entirely for a while. Competing responsibilities meant I often had to triage what got my attention, and sometimes the LMS lost that battle. The fatigue was both physical and mental, making it hard to engage meaningfully even when I tried.
Q14: Many times I'd open readings and scroll through them without really processing the content because my mind was elsewhere. Sometimes I'd watch discussion threads update without contributing because I didn't have the mental energy to formulate responses. There were sessions where I logged in, clicked around to feel like I was doing something, but learned nothing. My body was present but my mind was dealing with stress and sadness.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly even though I knew they were hurting my grade. The anxiety around starting them was sometimes worse than the anxiety of not doing them, which sounds irrational but that's how it felt. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow when I felt better, but tomorrow I'd feel the same. Some tasks felt so overwhelming that avoidance became a coping mechanism, even though it wasn't a good one.
Q16: Deadlines were both my enemy and my savior. They created intense stress and anxiety, but they were also the only thing that forced me to actually complete work. I'd often have bursts of LMS activity right before something was due, cramming weeks of engagement into hours. Without deadlines, I probably would have drifted even more. The pressure was unhealthy but necessary for my productivity.
Q17: When I received positive feedback on exams, it gave me a temporary boost that made me want to engage more. But feedback on assignments was often discouraging given my lower scores there, which made me want to avoid similar tasks. Constructive criticism sometimes helped me focus my studying, but harsh feedback increased my anxiety. The timing mattered too—feedback that came too late felt pointless.
Q18: The course structure with clear modules helped me understand what to prioritize. Having materials organized by week made it easier to plan my limited study time. However, when multiple things were due simultaneously, the structure felt suffocating rather than helpful. The peer support intervention was actually really valuable because it gave me human connection within the course structure, which helped with my sadness.
Q19: Having downloadable materials made engagement much easier given my internet situation. Clear expectations for assignments would have helped reduce my anxiety about them. The discussion forums were good but sometimes felt intimidating to post in when I wasn't confident. Shorter, more frequent low-stakes activities might have helped me build momentum instead of facing large overwhelming tasks.
Q20: I think that could reflect someone who is trying really hard but struggling with the actual output, maybe due to anxiety or confusion about what's expected. They might be searching for understanding, reading things multiple times, but still not feeling confident enough to submit work. Or they could be dealing with personal issues that make the final step of completion feel impossible. High activity doesn't always mean productive activity.
Q21: This actually sounds somewhat like me. I performed well on exams because I studied extensively offline, but my LMS activity was relatively low because that's not where my real learning happened. Someone with strong reading and writing skills might not need to watch every video or participate in every discussion to understand the material. The LMS captures presence, not necessarily the depth of learning happening elsewhere.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when someone logs in, what they click on, and whether they submit work on time. But they completely miss offline studying, emotional state while engaging, and the quality of understanding gained. They can't capture the hours I spent with handwritten notes or the conversations with peers outside the platform. Logs show behavior, not learning or the human experience behind the numbers.
Q23: Being engaged means genuinely caring about understanding the material and putting in effort to learn, even when it's hard. It's not just about clicking through content but actually wrestling with ideas and trying to make them your own. For me, engagement also includes the emotional investment—feeling connected to the course and believing it matters. True engagement survives bad days and setbacks, which requires resilience.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show the sadness I carried through much of the semester or how hard I had to fight against low motivation just to keep going. It wouldn't capture the peer support conversations that kept me from giving up, or the hours of offline studying that actually prepared me for exams. My high anxiety around assignments explains my low completion rate better than any click data could. The human story behind my numbers is invisible to the system.
